summary: Jeff Atwood explores the diminishing returns of file compression algorithms, sparked by his experience compressing Windows XP virtual machine images. He found that 7zip produced files 17% smaller than WinZip but took 7.3 times longer to complete. Using benchmark data, he demonstrates that beyond an optimal compression level, performance degrades dramatically while gains become marginal. The post concludes that the right compression choice depends on how often data is compressed, how many times it's transferred, and network speed—there's no universal best algorithm.
key_points: Beyond a certain compression level, performance falls off a cliff with rapidly diminishing returns
Compression is fundamentally a zero-sum game: you can have efficiency or speed, but not both
The decision of which compression level to use depends on compression frequency, transfer count, and network speed
File-type specific compression and solid archiving can dramatically outperform generic compression
Most compression benchmarks focus on ratios while ignoring the extreme time costs of maximum compression
money_quotes: Beyond a certain optimal compression level, performance falls off a cliff.
I do end up with a file that is 17% smaller, but it takes 7.3 times longer. That sure doesn't seem like a very good deal to me.
When it takes 5 times longer for barely 8% more compression, you've fallen off a cliff.
Compression is the zero-sum game it really is: you can either have efficiency, or you can have speed.
If you're holding out for Hyper Turbo Extreme Compression X algorithm, you may be waiting a while.
Some of the more extreme space-optimized compression algorithms can take several hours to compress the same file!
key_insight: Compression algorithms exhibit severe diminishing returns past an optimal point, and choosing the right compression level requires weighing time costs against actual transfer and storage needs rather than chasing maximum compression ratios.
